coredata.dc:title: Construction of optical dual-mode sensing platform based on green emissive carbon quantum dots for effective detection of ClO- and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Huajie
Li, Dai
Zheng, Yutao
wang, Kui
zhang, He
Feng, Zhipeng
Huang, Bolin
Wen, Huiyun
Wu, Jiyong
Xue, Weiming
Huang, Saipeng
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite (ClO-) is an important redox regulator in reactive oxygen species, which play a considerable role in oxidative stress and related diseases. Hence, accurate and sensitive monitoring of ClO- concentration was urgently needed in the fields of life sciences, food and environment. Bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) were synthesized utilizing one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid and acriflavine precursors. Through TEM, FTIR, XPS and zeta potential characterization procedures, G-CQDs illustrated uniformly dispersed and significant number of –NH2 and –OH on the surface. Meanwhile, the fluorescence and colorimetric analysis exhibited wide linear range and low detection limit response to ClO-. The fluorescence changes of G-CQDs were identified via smartphone to realize mobile sensing of ClO-. Subsequently, G-CQDs was applied for visualization and quantitative detection of ClO- in drinking water samples with satisfactory recovery rate. More importantly, G-CQDs demonstrated good water solubility, optical stability and excellent biocompatibility, which offered a promising analysis approach in cell imaging and exogenous ClO- detection in living cells. G-CQDs illustrated bright prospect and great potential in practical application of ClO- associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hypochlorite
Carbon quantum dots
Optical dual-mode
Smartphone-assisted
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric detection of ClO
Visualization determination of ClO- by smartphone imaging
Cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of ClO

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shanxi Province ( 2023-JC-YB-101 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (No. ZR 2022LSW023 ) and Basic Science Research Program of Shanxi Basic Sciences Institute (22JHQ079, Chemistry, Biology).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of G-CQDs. (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution) (B) FT-IR spectra of the G-CQDs. (C) Full XPS spectra of G-CQDs. High-resolution XPS of C 1 s peak (D), N 1 s peak (E) and O 1 s peak (F).

(A) UV–vis absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (green) spectra of the G-CQDs, respectively. (Inset: pictures of G-CQDs under 365 nm UV light (left) and sunlight (right)). (B)The CIE coordinates of G-CQDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of G-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra (A) and UV–vis absorption spectra (C) of G-CQDs with different concentrations of ClO-. (Inset: fluorescence and visualization images of G-CQDs after interaction with different ClO-). (B) Correlation between F0-F and ClO- concentrations (0–100 µM). (Inset: Linear relationship between F0-F and concentration of ClO- (0.1–60 µM)). (D) Linear relationship between A0-A and concentration of ClO- (0–200 µM) (Inset: Linear relationship between A0-A and concentration of ClO- (0–160 µM)).

The selectivity and anti-interference against inorganic salt ions (400 μM) or active species (100 μM). (A) The relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 of G-CQDs (60 μM ClO-); (B) The relative UV absorption intensity A/A0 of G-CQDs (200 μM ClO-).

(A) Fluorescence excitation (blue) and emission spectra (green) of G-CQDs and UV–vis absorption of ClO- (red) (B) Fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs (black) and G-CQDs + ClO- (red). (C) UV–vis absorbance spectra of G-CQDs (black), ClO- (blue) and G-CQDs + ClO- (red). (D) FT-IR spectra of G-CQDs (red) and G-CQDs + ClO- (black).

Schematic diagram of a smartphone-based color recognition app detecting ClO-. (A) Take G-CQDs images under 365 nm UV light (B) Record the RGB value of the G-CQDs image (C) Linear relationship between G value and ClO- concentrations (0–60 μM).
Confocal fluorescence images of LO2 cells incubated with G-CQDs and different concentrations of ClO-.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of G-CQDs and their application for detection of ClO- and cellular imaging.
Results for the determination of ClO- in drinking water sample.

head.ce:title: Construction of optical dual-mode sensing platform based on green emissive carbon quantum dots for effective detection of ClO- and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi’an, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Shandong Second Provincial General Hospital, Jinan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Shandong Second Provincial General Hospital, Jinan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypochlorite (ClO-) is an important redox regulator in reactive oxygen species, which play a considerable role in oxidative stress and related diseases. Hence, accurate and sensitive monitoring of ClO- concentration was urgently needed in the fields of life sciences, food and environment. Bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) were synthesized utilizing one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid and acriflavine precursors. Through TEM, FTIR, XPS and zeta potential characterization procedures, G-CQDs illustrated uniformly dispersed and significant number of –NH2 and –OH on the surface. Meanwhile, the fluorescence and colorimetric analysis exhibited wide linear range and low detection limit response to ClO-. The fluorescence changes of G-CQDs were identified via smartphone to realize mobile sensing of ClO-. Subsequently, G-CQDs was applied for visualization and quantitative detection of ClO- in drinking water samples with satisfactory recovery rate. More importantly, G-CQDs demonstrated good water solubility, optical stability and excellent biocompatibility, which offered a promising analysis approach in cell imaging and exogenous ClO- detection in living cells. G-CQDs illustrated bright prospect and great potential in practical application of ClO- associated disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite is considered as an important reactive oxygen species, which is closely related to numerous physiological and pathological processes within organisms [1,2]. In addition, hypochlorite can be utilized as a disinfectant and preservative for sanitizing the environment and preserving food over extended periods to inhibit growth and reproduction of bacteria [3,4]. However, excessive ClO-, which originated from the circulation of the food chain and accumulation within organisms, can disturb the redox balance and bring a severe threat to human health, exemplified by respiratory irritation [5], blood diseases [6], lung damage [7], atherosclerosis [8], cancer [9] and so on. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a simple, facile, highly efficient and selective analytical approach for ClO- detection in various conditions especially in biological systems, which will illustrate profound significance and practical value for ClO- related disease prevention and early clinical diagnosis (see Scheme 1 ).

Owing to the vital physiological and pathological effects of ClO-, many reasonable analytical methods have been developed to detect ClO-, such as high performance liquid chromatography [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [11], colorimetry [12], electrochemical method [13] and fluorescence method [14,15]. Compared with the above analytical methods, fluorescence and colorimetry have attracted much more attention recently due to their accessibility, easy preparation, non-invasion, high sensitivity and good compatibility for bio-samples. Therefore, dual-mode methods illustrated significant potential and application prospect for analysis and detection.

As a newest generation of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) illustrated many superior characteristics, comprising simple preparation approaches, stable chemical properties, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Consequently, CQDs were extensively utilized in food safety, environmental governance, bioimaging, analysis in living body and other optical sensing [16–18]. Currently, several researchers have employed CQDs as fluorescent nanoprobes to detect ClO-. For example, Gao et al. [19] prepared a CQDs with bright yellow fluorescence by a one-pot hydrothermal method to realize the dual-function identification of trace ClO- and S2O4 2-. Meng et al. [20] developed a CQDs with long-wavelength emission, low toxicity and good cell penetration, which was applied to monitor ClO- in living cells; Zhou et al. [21] proposed a CQDs with both green and red emission, and cleverly constructed a ratio fluorescence sensing platform for detecting ClO- in living organisms. Although these CQDs-based fluorescence sensors illustrated high sensitivity, long emission and double emission fluorescence, they were limited to obtaining detection results just by single fluorescence signal, which is easy to be misled by various interfering factors from complex sample environment and difficult to avoid the occurrence of false results. Hence, double signal or multi-signal analysis strategy was proposed to detect ClO-, which can be mutually verified to greatly improve the detection reliability. Bu et al. [22] prepared green CQDs by one-step solid-state reaction method, and detected ClO- in water samples by fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode methods. However, the above methods illustrated some disadvantages, such as complex operation, high cost and cannot be detected immediately.

Recently, smartphones-assisted visualization analytical application has been gradually evolved and matured in food safety, environmental monitoring and other field [23,24], which can effectively avoid errors provoked by color changes by naked eye. More importantly, the fluorescence and colorimetry binary channels method can rapidly detect target substance after the acquisition, recognition and RGB data processing under UV light, which can also greatly improve the analysis accuracy and reliability. Hence, the development of an optical dual-mode sensor based on smartphone imaging for ClO- detection was of great significance.

Green emissive carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) were synthetized by one-step hydrothermal method utilizing citric acid and acriflavine as raw materials. Then, a dual-mode optical sensor platform with fluorescence and colorimetric analysis was constructed based on G-CQDs for ultra-sensitive and selective detection of ClO- in drinking water. Meanwhile, smartphone imaging is utilized to quickly identify the fluorescence brightness change in visualization images under ultraviolet light. It is worth noting that G-CQDs can also be utilized for intracellular ClO- imaging and extrinsic ClO- detection, which attributed to excellent water solubility, high stability and low cytotoxicity. In conclusion, this work was expected to not only provide a more portable and reliable detection scheme for ClO- in drinking water, but also provided great opportunities for ClO- detection in the body.

The chemical reagents utilized in this experiment were all analytical grade. Citric acid (CA) and Acriflavine were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All inorganic salts (KCl, NaCl, ZnCl2, NiCl2, FeCl2、PbCl2, MnCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, NaF, NaNO3, NaHCO3, CH3COONa, Na2SeO3, NaH2PO4, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, Na2C2O4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium hypochlorite solution (Available chlorine content: 4.0%) was obtained from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydroxyl radical（·OH）was synthesized through the Fenton reaction with Fe2+ and excess H2O2, Singlet oxygen（1O2）was prepared from the reaction between NaClO and excess H2O2. Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) was generated by reaction of NaNO2 with excess H2O2. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) was provided by Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All ultrapure water required for sample preparation was produced by Milli-Q system (18.25 MΩ·cm, Millipore Co., USA).

UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were recorded by UV-2310 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer and F-970 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of G-CQDs were observed with Tecnai G2F20 microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was analyzed on a Frontier FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with the Thermo Escalab 250xi Spectrometer. The zeta potential of G-CQDs in aqueous solution was determined by Nano-ZS90 Malvern laser particle size analyzer. The fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs was tested using Edinburgh FLS1000. Cell imaging was performed by confocal fluorescence laser scanning microscope (Olympus, FV1000). All G-CQDs images in the ultraviolet were taken with a smartphone (Redmi Note 10 Pro).

Citric acid (2 g, 9.52 mmol) and Acriflavine (10.4 mg, 0.04 mmol) were put into 20 mL ultrapure water and sonicated to dissolution. The resulting solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reactant was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a 0.22 μm cellulose microporous membrane to remove insoluble solids. For further purification, the filtrate was dialyzed (MWCO = 500 Da) for 24 h to eliminate small molecules and impurities. Finally, the solid powder of G-CQDs was prepared through vacuum freeze-drying. The quantitative powder was uniformly dispersed in ultrapure water to obtain the G-CQDs stock solution (8 mg/mL), which was stored at 4 °C for further experiment.

All the fluorescence and colorimetric analysis was performed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4). In the fluorescence detection of ClO-, 100 μL G-CQDs stock solution was diluted with 1.8 mL buffer solution in a centrifuge tube to prepare a series probe solution. Subsequently, 100 μL ClO- with different concentrations were added into the above diluent solution, resulting in a final concentration of ClO- ranging from 0 to 100 μM, which were incubated at room temperature for 1 min and then optical change were measured by fluorescence spectrometer.

The colorimetric detection of ClO- was investigated by a probe solution, which mixed 300 uL G-CQDs stock solution with 1.6 mL buffer solution. Under the above conditions, probe solution was responded with ClO- in a final concentration range of 0–200 uM and the UV–visible spectrum was scanned.

The G-CQDs solution was responded and reacted with different concentrations of ClO-. The mixed solution was placed in a fixed angle and position under 365 nm UV light. Next, the optical recognition App on a smartphone was utilized to scan and determine the color and fluorescence change, which was subsequently converted to the corresponding RGB value (R: red, G: green, B: blue). Finally, the linear relationship between RGB value and ClO- concentration was fitted to achieve convenient and visual detection of ClO- concentration.

LO2 cells were seeded in a cell culture dish with DMEM medium at 37 °C for 24 h, which was washed twice with PBS solution and incubated with G-CQDs solution (40 μg/mL) for 4 h. Then, the LO2 cells were cultured with different concentrations ClO- aqueous solution for 30 min. Finally, the intracellular ClO- were analyzed by confocal microscopy at 460 nm after washing with PBS for 3 times.

The morphology and size of G-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 A). The TEM images revealed that G-CQDs were spherical in shape, uniformly distributed, and had an average diameter of 2.11 ± 0.68 nm. Furthermore, the HRTEM image (Fig. S1) demonstrated a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphitic carbon [25], indicating successful carbonization of G-CQDs.

As shown in Fig. S2, the zeta potential of G-CQDs in aqueous solution was 10.88 mV, implying the possible presence of positively charged amino groups on the surface of G-CQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were utilized to investigate the chemical composition and surface functional groups of G-CQDs. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B), the characteristic absorption peaks of G-CQDs at 3495 cm−1 and 3284 cm−1 were correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH [26]. The asymmetric stretching vibration peak of carbonyl (CO) was found at 1685 cm−1 [27]. Additionally, the presence of two spikes at 1390 cm−1 and 1309 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-N, illustrating the successful doping of N in G-CQDs [28]. The absorption peaks at 2924 cm−1, 2360 cm−1 and 1211 cm−1 were closely related to the bending vibrations of CH, OCO- and CO, respectively [19,29]. Additionally, significant differences in stretching vibrations were observed compared with the FTIR spectrum of G-CQDs and acriflavine, which further confirmed the formation of new substance.

From the XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C), it can be observed that G-CQDs are mainly composed of C, N and O elements, whose atomic ratios are 58.2%, 2.44% and 39.33%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) exhibited five peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.2 eV, 288.4 eV, and 289.9 eV, typical peaks of CC, CC, CN, CO, and CO groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectroscopy (Fig. 1E) illustrated the presence of three types of nitrogen dopants, namely pyridinic N (398.9 eV), graphitic N (399.9 eV) and amino N (401.8 eV). The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) indicated three peaks at 532.1, 532.4, and 533.2 eV, which correspond to the CO, CO, and COH/COC bonds, respectively [21,30]. In brief, the XPS spectrum demonstrated abundant NH2, OH, COOH and other functional groups on the surface of G-CQDs, which indicates that G-CQDs have great water solubility and can be easily oxidized by ClO-.

The optical properties of G-CQDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 A (inset), the diluted G-CQDs solution displayed a faint yellow color in daylight and a vivid green fluorescence in 365 nm UV light. As demonstrated in the UV spectrum (Fig. 2A), the G-CQDs exhibited two absorption peaks around 262 nm and 290 nm, which originated from π-π* transition in the aromatic sp2 structural domain. Meanwhile, a broad peak was found around 451 nm, which corresponded to n-π* transition of CO/CN [31]. In the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2A), the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CQDs were 460 nm and 515 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectrum data of G-CQDs were imported into the CIE diagram. The coordinate of maximum emission intensity was (0.2429, 0.5926) in the CIE chromaticity diagram, further indicating the green fluorescence of G-CQDs (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, when the excitation wavelength was shifted from 400 nm to 500 nm, the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased, while the emission wavelength remained constant (Fig. 2C). The results indicated that G-CQDs independently maintained unique emission center and excitation wavelength, which is closely related to the uniform surface structure [32,33]. Finally, the fluorescence quantum yield of G-CQDs was calculated as 13.09 % by quinine sulfate as reference (in the ESI).

Next, in order to explore the influence of external environment on optical stability, some relevant experiments were designed and carried out. In the NaCl concentration range of 10-5 − 1.0 M, the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs remained basically unchanged and constant, indicating excellent resistance against anion and cation interference (Fig. S3). When G-CQDs were exposed under xenon lamp for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity fluctuated only slightly, demonstrating that G-CQDs have strong photobleaching resistance (Fig. S4). Similarly, G-CQDs had good stability in high temperature environments, even up to 60 °C (Fig. S5). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs was almost constant while storing in a dry, light-free environment at 4 °C for 30 days (Fig. S6). In conclusion, the above experiment demonstrated G-CQDs possessed excellent photobleaching resistance, chemical and optical stability, which laid a strong foundation for fluorescence detection and biological imaging in complex sample systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric detection of ClO
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the detection conditions of G-CQDs for ClO-, the influence factors of response time and pH were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs apparently quenched within 1 min after the addition of ClO- and remained stable within 30 min, which illustrated the response time. According to Fig. S8, the quenching efficiency of ClO- for G-CQDs was the best in acidic buffer solution, which the oxidative property was stronger in weakly acidic environment. However, considering the effective sensing of ClO- in biological system, buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4) was finally selected for subsequent experiments.

As displayed in Fig. 3 A, with the continuous increase of ClO- concentration (0–60 μM), the green emission peak of G-CQDs gradually decreased, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching degree of G-CQDs may be positively correlated with the concentration of ClO-. Further, Fig. 3B exhibited that the linear regression equation of F0-F and ClO- concentration (0.1–60.0 μM) is F0-F = 93.1562 [ClO-] + 62.1989 (R2 = 0.995), where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs in the presence and absence of ClO-, respectively. Finally, the limit of detection was calculated as low as 76.2 nM according to the equation based on 3δ/k, where “σ” is the standard deviation, and “k” is the slope of the calibration curve.

In addition, the effect of ClO- on the UV absorbance of G-CQDs was also examined. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, when the concentration of ClO- increased, the UV absorption of G-CQDs at 451 nm gradually linearly weaken and the color change of G-CQDs can be observed from light yellow to colorless by naked eye. Therefore, the linear relationship between UV absorbance and ClO- concentration can be established. As revealed from Fig. 3D, the UV absorbance of G-CQDs and ClO- illustrated a good linear relationship, while the ClO- concentration ranged from 0 to 160 μM. The linear equation was A0-A = 0.00286 [ClO-] + 0.0118 (R2 = 0.998), where A0 and A are the respective absorbance of G-CQDs at 451 nm without ClO- and with ClO-, respectively. The detection limit was calculated to be 1.28 μM according to the rule of 3δ/k. In conclusion, G-CQDs can recognize ClO- through dual signals channel and improve the sensor reliability through interactive verification.

The selectivity and anti-interference of G-CQDs were also important factors in evaluating sensing systems. Twenty different inorganic salt ions (K+, Na+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Al3+, F-, Cl-, NO3 –, HCO3 –, CH3COO–, SeO3 2-, H2PO4 2-, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, C2O4 2-) and four active species (H2O2, ·OH, ONOO−, 1O2) on the optical properties of G-CQDs were also investigated. It was found that that only ClO- can significantly quench the fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance of G-CQDs. Meanwhile, the images under 365 nm UV light and sunlight can further confirm this phenomenon (Fig. S9). More importantly, when interfering ions were added to the mixture of G-CQDs and ClO-, the influence of G-CQDs on ClO- sensing was also negligible (Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B). Therefore, it can be concluded that G-CQDs exhibit potential practicality as a fluorescent probe for detecting ClO- in complex practical samples.

At present, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of carbon quantum dots was widely explained by different theories, for example static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filtration effect (IFE), or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [34]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CQDs was explored through UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectrum, fluorescence lifetime and FT-IR spectrum. Firstly, the UV–vis absorption spectra of ClO- were compared with the excitation and emission spectra of G-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the UV–vis absorption peak of ClO- was in the range of 200–350 nm, which didn't overlap the excitation and emission peaks of G-CQDs, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of G-CQDs is independent of the internal filtration effect (IFE) and Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [35]. According to Fig. 5B, the average fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs decreased from 4.69 ns to 3.51 ns when ClO- (100 μM) was added. Therefore, the mechanism initially was assessed as dynamic quenching of G-CQDs induced by ClO- [36].

Meanwhile, the Stern-Volmer plots was generated to observe the fluorescence quenching of G-CQDs at different ClO- concentrations. Fig. S10 demonstrated that F0/F-1 illustrated a linear correlation with the ClO- concentration at low range (0.1–30 μM), while the fitting curve exhibited an upward curvature at high concentrations range. The above results indicated that the quenching process is related to static quenching and dynamic quenching corresponding with the existing literature [21,37]. The linear fraction in a low concentration range (ClO-: 0.1–30 μM) was examined by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
F
- 1 =
K
sv
[ Q ]
=
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]

In this equation, F0 and F represents the fluorescence intensity in the absence ClO- and presence of ClO-, respectively. Ksv denotes the quenching constant. τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs; Kq is the quenching rate constant; Q is the concentration of ClO-. According to the Stern-Volmer curve slope (Ksv = 2.18 × 104 M−1) and the fluorescence lifetime of G-CQDs (τ0 = 4.69 ns), the Kq for the process of fluorescence quenching was 4.65 × 1012 M−1S−1, which considerably exceeded the limit value of 1 × 1010 M−1 S-1 and confirmed the existence of static quenching during fluorescence recognition [38]. Subsequently, the UV–visible absorption of G-CQDs, ClO- solution and G-CQDs + ClO- were further investigated. Fig. 5C illustrated that the UV–vis absorption peak of G-CQDs at 262 nm disappeared after exposing in ClO- at a concentration of 100 µM. Similarly, the UV–vis absorption peak at 451 nm weakened and shifted to the right, indicating that the transformation of G-CQDs from light yellow to colorless, which may be ascribed to the surface functional groups of G-CQDs were oxidized by ClO- [39].

Meanwhile, ninhydrin chromogenic method was utilized to estimate the presence of amino groups in Fig. S11, the G-CQDs + ninhydrin indicated deep brown color, while the G-CQDs + ClO- + ninhydrin didn't display any colors, which further proved that the amino groups on the surface of G-CQDs was oxidized . More significantly, the FT-IR spectra of G-CQDs demonstrated alterations in the position and intensity of OH/NH tensile vibration peaks (3495 and 3284 cm−1) the presence of ClO- (Fig. 5D). Furthermore, significant variations in the absorption peaks of C-N/C-O ranging from 1400 to 1100 cm−1 indicated oxidation of the groups on the surface of G-CQDs, which was considered as potential destruction of the fluorescence chromophore framework [40,41]. Consequently, the above experimental and characterization results indicated that the fluorescence quenching and reduction of UV–vis absorption in G-CQDs are co-regulation of static and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization determination of ClO- by smartphone imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above experiments results, the green fluorescence of G-CQDs is closely linearly correlated to the concentration of ClO-. However, the color resolution by naked eye was limited and prone to error, which hindered the accuracy of visualization assessment. The RGB value was established to investigate the correlation between the color variation of G-CQDs and the concentration of ClO- via the color recognition application on the smartphone. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the green fluorescence of G-CQDs decreased gradually as the ClO- concentration increased under 365 nm UV light, which established an essential prerequisite and foundation for visual recognition. Subsequently, the RGB values of G-CQDs in the image were extracted and recorded by the color recognition App (Fig. 6B). Based on the G value decreases systematically with the increase of ClO- concentration, the working curve of G value and ClO- concentration was fitted. As portrayed in Fig. 6C, the linear equation of G value and ClO- concentration was G value = -0.82[ClO-] + 181.28 (R2 = 0.996), and the detection limit (LOD) calculated from 3δ/k is as low as 6.56 μM.

Meanwhile, to confirm the analysis applicability and effectiveness of G-CQD in complex samples, drinking water was selected as the actual sample for the analysis of ClO-. As shown in Table 1 , after adding different concentrations of ClO-, the recoveries of fluorescence method and UV–visible absorbance method were 97.62 % − 101.83 %, RSD ≤ 1.81 % and 98.66 % − 104.93 %, RSD ≤ 3.29 %, respectively. In addition, the recovery rate based on G-CQDs intelligent sensing platform is 96.61 % − 99.25 %, RSD ≤ 6.15 %. In summary, the smartphone-assisted G-CQDs sensing platform successfully provided a simple and convenient strategy for ClO- detection, which avoids the complexity and operational deviation from large analytical instruments. Compared with the previous work of the predecessors in Table S1, the sensor not only illustrated a lower detection limit, but also exhibited a variety of detection channels for interactive verification, which greatly improved the accuracy and reliability of the analysis results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cellular imaging of ClO
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cytotoxicity of G-CQDs, LO2 cells were incubated with various concentrations of G-CQDs for 24 h utilizing the CCK-8 method. Notably, the viability of LO2 cells remained around 90% even at concentrations as high as 100 µg/mL of G-CQDs (Fig. S12). Therefore, G-CQDs illustrated low toxicity and biocompatibility, which provides a great potential for monitoring ClO- in complex biological systems.

Next, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was utilized to investigate the ability of G-CQDs as a probe to search trace ClO- in living cells. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , under excitation of 460 nm, LO2 cells incubated with 40 µg/mL G-CQDs can be observed to exhibit bright green fluorescence signals with distinct outlines and complete morphology, which suggested that G-CQDs have excellent cell permeability and can effectively perform cell imaging. More importantly, with the increase of exogenous ClO- concentration, the fluorescence intensity of LO2 cells gradually decreased, which corresponded with the analysis result and trend in vitro. This experiment demonstrated that G-CQDs can potentially be utilized to monitor ClO- in vivo, offered analysis guidance in the early diagnosis and treatment of ClO- related diseases.

In summary, G-CQDs were prepared via a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal method with citric acid and acriflavine as raw materials. Subsequently, a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode analytical platform was constructed based on G-CQDs for specific detection of ClO- in drinking water. Then, the convenient sensing of ClO- was realized through smartphone-assisted recognition of fluorescent color change. All the above methods illustrated the advantages of wide detection range, low detection limit and small error. In addition, G-CQDs also has been successfully utilized for the recognition of exogenous ClO- in living cells due to its excellent optical stability and low cytotoxicity. In brief, G-CQDs are expected to provide a reliable and practical strategy in the fields of food safety, environmental governance and human health monitoring.

Huajie Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Dai Li: Investigation, Visualization. Yutao Zheng: Investigation, Methodology. Kui wang: Investigation, Validation. He zhang: Investigation, Validation. Zhipeng Feng: Data curation, Investigation. Bolin Huang: Data curation, Investigation. Huiyun Wen: Writing – review & editing. Jiyong Wu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Weiming Xue: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Saipeng Huang: Data curation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shanxi Province (2023-JC-YB-101), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (No. ZR 2022LSW023) and Basic Science Research Program of Shanxi Basic Sciences Institute (22JHQ079, Chemistry, Biology).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123733.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
